title: Mutation, Alleles and Genetic Change
subtitle: Where genuinely new alleles come from
subject: Biology
syllabusDotPoints: compare the causes, processes and effects of different types of mutation, including but not limited to point and chromosomal mutation
investigate the causes of genetic variation relating to the processes of fertilisation, meiosis and mutation (ACSBL078)
inquiryQuestion: How does mutation introduce new alleles into a population?
lessonIntent: Students can explain that mutation is the source of new alleles, while meiosis and fertilisation only reshuffle alleles that already exist.
examSkill: Classify a described example as new allele creation, allele reshuffling, or selection changing frequency.

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Biology. Today, mutation, alleles and genetic change. Let's get into it.

scenes.caption: Mutation, Alleles and Genetic Change (Year 12 Biology, Module 6, Lesson 1)

scenes.heading: The superbug that was already there
scenes.body: Resistant bacteria did not decide to fight back. The resistant allele was already in the population, waiting.
scenes.callout: Did selection create resistance, or just choose from variants that already existed?
scenes.caption: Antibiotic resistance arose from pre-existing mutations, not purposeful change.

voiceover.text: Back in nineteen forty five, Alexander Fleming stood up to accept his Nobel Prize, and he gave a warning. If people misused penicillin, he said, they would breed resistant bacteria. He was right. By twenty nineteen, the World Health Organization reported that one drug-resistant superbug alone was driving over seven hundred thousand deaths a year. Now here's the part students get wrong. The bacteria did not sense the antibiotic and decide to fight back. A random mutation that happened to give resistance was already sitting in some cells, before the drug ever arrived. The antibiotic just killed everything else and let the resistant ones take over. So today we separate two completely different ideas, making a brand new allele, versus simply reshuffling alleles that already exist.

scenes.heading: Mutation is the only source of new alleles
scenes.body: A mutation is a change in the DNA sequence. Because a gene is a DNA sequence, a mutation can produce a genuinely new version of that gene.

bullets.text: Mutation changes the DNA, so it can create an allele that never existed before.
Meiosis and fertilisation only shuffle alleles that are already present.
Mutation is random with respect to need, the environment does not order it up.

scenes.secondary: Module 5 was mostly about inheriting alleles that already exist. Module 6 asks the deeper question, where do new alleles come from in the first place.
scenes.callout: New allele means new DNA. Only mutation does that.
scenes.caption: Mutation creates new alleles; it is random with respect to need.

voiceover.text: Let's nail the central idea. A mutation is a change in the DNA sequence, and since a gene is just a stretch of DNA, changing it can produce a brand new version of that gene, which we call a new allele. This is the only process that introduces genuinely new genetic information into a population. Hold on to that, because meiosis and fertilisation feel like they create new things, but they don't, they only shuffle alleles that are already there. The other key phrase is this, mutation is random with respect to need. The environment never instructs an organism to make exactly the change it needs. The mutation happens first, by chance. Then, much later, if that allele helps survival, natural selection might make it more common. So the order matters, mutation comes first, selection comes afterward, and they are two separate steps.

scenes.heading: The words you need

bullets.text: Mutation: a change in the DNA sequence; the source of new alleles.
Allele: a variant form of a gene, found at the same locus on homologous chromosomes.
Gene pool: the total collection of alleles present in a population.
Genetic variation: the differences in genetic makeup between individuals in a population.

scenes.callout: Gene, allele, gene pool: three different levels. Don't blur them.
scenes.caption: Mutation, allele, gene pool, genetic variation.

voiceover.text: Let's pin down the vocabulary, because mixing these up is where marks vanish. A mutation is a change in the DNA sequence, and it's the source of new alleles. An allele is a variant form of a gene, sitting at the same locus, the same address, on homologous chromosomes. A gene pool is bigger, it's the total collection of alleles present across a whole population, not in one organism. And genetic variation is simply the differences in genetic makeup between individuals. Notice the three levels here. A gene is the named region, an allele is one specific version of it, and the gene pool is all the alleles in the population added together. Examiners love to catch students who slide between these levels, so keep them distinct.

scenes.heading: Meiosis and fertilisation reshuffle, they don't create
scenes.body: Meiosis and fertilisation generate huge variation, but normally only by recombining alleles that already exist in the parents.

bullets.text: Meiosis: independent assortment and crossing over make genetically different gametes.
Fertilisation: random fusion of gametes makes new allele combinations in offspring.
Neither one writes a new DNA sequence, so neither makes a new allele.

scenes.secondary: This is the heart of the inquiry question. Mutation supplies novelty. Meiosis and fertilisation spread and recombine what is already there.
scenes.callout: New combination is not the same as new allele.
scenes.caption: Meiosis and fertilisation recombine existing alleles; only mutation makes new ones.

voiceover.text: So if mutation makes new alleles, what exactly do meiosis and fertilisation do? They generate an enormous amount of variation, but in a different way. Back in Module 5 you saw that meiosis shuffles the deck, through independent assortment of chromosomes and crossing over between them, so each gamete ends up genetically different. Then fertilisation combines one gamete from each parent at random, creating fresh combinations in the offspring. Here's the crucial point, though. Neither process writes a single new letter of DNA. They are rearranging alleles that the parents already carried. That's why two siblings can look completely different without any new mutation, it's all reshuffling. So a new combination is not the same thing as a new allele, and that distinction is the whole inquiry question in one line.

scenes.heading: New allele, reshuffle, or selection?
scenes.question: Classify each: (a) a single DNA base change produces a gene variant never seen before; (b) two siblings inherit different combinations of their parents' alleles; (c) after antibiotic treatment, resistant bacteria become much more common.
scenes.coachNote: Ask: did the DNA sequence change (mutation), were existing alleles rearranged (meiosis/fertilisation), or did an allele become more common (selection)?
steps: (a) A new base sequence means a new allele, so this is mutation.
(b) Different combinations of existing alleles, no new DNA, so this is meiosis and fertilisation reshuffling.
(c) An allele that already existed becomes more common, so this is selection changing frequency.
Decision rule: new DNA is mutation; rearranged alleles is reshuffling; a shift in how common an allele is, is selection.
scenes.caption: (a) mutation, (b) reshuffling, (c) selection changing frequency.

voiceover.text: Let's run three scenarios through the test. Part a, a single DNA base changes and produces a gene variant never seen before in the population. The DNA sequence itself is different, so a brand new allele exists, that's mutation. Part b, two siblings inherit different combinations of their parents' alleles. Nothing new has been written into the DNA, the alleles already existed in mum and dad, they've just been dealt out in different combinations. That's meiosis and fertilisation reshuffling. Part c, after antibiotic treatment, resistant bacteria become much more common. The resistant allele was already there, it hasn't been created, it's just become more frequent because the susceptible bacteria died. That's selection changing frequency. So the rule is simple, new DNA means mutation, rearranged alleles means reshuffling, and a change in how common an allele is means selection.

scenes.heading: The big traps
scenes.body: Trap 1: saying bacteria mutate so they can survive. Mutation is random with respect to need; the antibiotic just selects survivors afterward.
scenes.secondary: Trap 2: thinking every mutation changes the phenotype. Most mutations land in non-coding DNA or make a synonymous codon, so they have no visible effect. Trap 3: saying meiosis or fertilisation creates new alleles. They only recombine existing ones.
scenes.callout: Mutation first, then selection. Random, not on demand.
scenes.caption: Mutation is random, not purposeful; most mutations are silent; reshuffling is not creation.

voiceover.text: Three traps that cost marks every single year. Trap one, the big one, writing that bacteria mutate so they can survive. That sentence reverses cause and effect. Mutation is random with respect to need, the resistant allele appears by chance first, and only then does the antibiotic act as a filter that keeps the survivors. The mutation is not a response to the drug. Trap two, assuming every mutation changes the phenotype. Actually most mutations land in non-coding DNA, or they produce a synonymous codon that still codes for the same amino acid, so they have no visible effect at all. Only mutations that alter protein structure or gene regulation tend to change the phenotype. And trap three, saying meiosis or fertilisation creates new alleles. They don't, they only recombine alleles that already exist. Get those three right and these questions become free marks.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: A student writes: 'A population evolved resistance because the individuals mutated when they needed to.' Identify the error and rewrite the sentence using accurate Module 6 language.
scenes.pausePrompt: Pause and fix it before the answer.
answerSteps: The error is treating mutation as purposeful, as if it happens because it is needed.
Mutation is random with respect to need; the resistant allele arose by chance, independent of the threat.
Natural selection then increased the frequency of that pre-existing allele once the environment changed.
Rewrite: random mutation produced a resistant allele, and natural selection increased its frequency under selection pressure.
Answer: mutation supplies the allele by chance; selection, not need, makes it common.
scenes.caption: Mutation is random; selection raises the frequency of pre-existing alleles.

voiceover.text: Quick check. A student writes, a population evolved resistance because the individuals mutated when they needed to. Pause and fix that sentence before I do. Right, let's break it. The error is treating mutation as if it's purposeful, as if it happens on demand because the organism needs it. That's not how it works. Mutation is random with respect to need, the resistant allele arose by chance, completely independent of whether any threat was present. What actually changes the population is natural selection. Once the environment shifts, selection increases the frequency of that pre-existing allele. So a clean rewrite would be, a random mutation produced a resistant allele, and natural selection then increased its frequency under selection pressure. Mutation supplies the raw material by chance, and selection, not need, is what makes it common.

scenes.heading: Lock it in
points: A mutation is a change in DNA, and it is the only source of genuinely new alleles.
Meiosis and fertilisation reshuffle existing alleles into new combinations, they do not create new ones.
Gene, allele and gene pool are three different levels, keep them distinct.
Mutation is random with respect to need; selection acts afterward.
Most mutations are silent, only some change the phenotype.
scenes.finalPrompt: Classifying a change? Ask: new DNA, rearranged alleles, or a shift in frequency?
scenes.caption: Five takeaways: mutation makes alleles, reshuffling spreads them, random not purposeful.

voiceover.text: Let's lock it in. Five things to take away. First, a mutation is a change in DNA, and it is the only source of genuinely new alleles. Second, meiosis and fertilisation reshuffle alleles that already exist into new combinations, they never create new ones. Third, gene, allele and gene pool are three separate levels, the gene is the region, the allele is a version, and the gene pool is all the alleles across a population. Fourth, mutation is random with respect to need, and selection only acts afterward. And fifth, most mutations are actually silent, only some of them change the phenotype. Whenever you classify a genetic change, just ask, is this new DNA, rearranged alleles, or a shift in frequency. See you next lesson.
